docstring:
A *skewed stability function* on a thin subcategory `P((a, b))` with `b - a ≤ 1`.
This is a group homomorphism `W : Λ →+ ℂ` (the charge on the target lattice) together
with a class map `v : K₀ C →+ Λ` and a real parameter `α ∈ (a, b)`, such that for
every nonzero semistable object `E` of phase `φ ∈ (a, b)`, `W(v[E]) ≠ 0`.

In the deformation theorem, `W` is a perturbation of the central charge `Z : Λ → ℂ`
of a stability condition `(Z, P)` on `D` with respect to `Λ`, and `α` is chosen so
that `W`-phases are well-defined in `(α - 1/2, α + 1/2)` for objects in `P((a, b))`.

The central charge of a `SkewedStabilityFunction` is additive on strict short exact
sequences in the thin interval category.

A strict short exact sequence in `P((a,b))` yields the expected `K₀` relation
in the ambient triangulated category.
Strict epimorphisms in `P((a,b))` are closed under composition.
Strict monomorphisms in `P((a,b))` are closed under composition.

A short exact sequence in the left heart `P((a,a+1])` with vertices in `P((a,b))`
extends to a distinguished triangle in `C`.
A strict short exact sequence in `P((a,b))` extends to a distinguished triangle in `C`.

Finite subobjects in the left heart transfer to finite subobjects in `P((a,b))`
via the fully faithful left-heart embedding.

A distinguished triangle in `C` whose three vertices lie in `P((a,b))`
forces the first map to be a strict monomorphism and the second to be a strict epimorphism
in `P((a,b))`.

If a short complex in `P((a,b))` is short exact after embedding into both hearts,
then its left map is a strict monomorphism and its right map is a strict epimorphism
in `P((a,b))`.

Strict short exact sequences in `P((a,b))` are exactly the distinguished triangles
in `C` whose three vertices lie in `P((a,b))`.

A strict short exact sequence in a smaller interval remains strict in any larger thin
interval containing it. This is the inclusion-case transport used in the deformation
theorem's interval-independence step.

A distinguished triangle in `C` whose three vertices lie in `P((a,b))`
defines a strict short exact sequence in `P((a,b))`.

paperComment: per-object strict finite length is weaker than finite length of all chains (paper's assumption)
docstring:
A slicing is locally finite if there exists `η > 0` with `η < 1/2` such that every
object in each thin interval category `P((t-η, t+η))` has finite length in the
quasi-abelian sense, i.e. ACC/DCC on strict subobjects.

The extra bound `η < 1/2` is a harmless normalization: any Bridgeland witness may be
shrunk to such an `η`, and then the width `2η` is at most `1`, so the thin interval
category carries the exact / quasi-abelian structure proved above.

A strict epimorphism in `P((a, b))` becomes an epimorphism in the right heart
`P([b - 1, b))`.

A strict monomorphism in `P((a, b))` becomes a monomorphism in the left heart
`P((a, a + 1])`.

The cokernel of a morphism in `P((a,b))` is computed by the right heart
`P([b - 1, b))`.
The left ambient abelian heart `P((a,a+1])`.
The right ambient abelian heart `P([b-1,b))`.

A strict epimorphism in `P((a, b))` becomes an epimorphism in the left heart
`P((a, a + 1])`.

If a morphism in `P((a, b))` becomes an epimorphism in the left heart
`P((a, a + 1])`, then it is a strict epimorphism in `P((a, b))`.

If a morphism in `P((a, b))` becomes a monomorphism in the right heart
`P([b - 1, b))`, then it is a strict monomorphism in `P((a, b))`.

The kernel of a morphism in `P((a,b))` is computed by the left heart
`P((a,a+1])`.
The fully faithful embedding of `P((a,b))` into the left heart `P((a,a+1])`.
The fully faithful embedding of `P((a,b))` into the right heart `P([b-1,b))`.

**Kernel/image containment in thin intervals.** In a distinguished triangle
`K → E → Q → K[1]` where `E ∈ P((a,b))` and both `K` and `Q` satisfy basic phase
bounds (`gtProp a` and `leProp (a+1)`), then `K ∈ P((a, b))`.

The upper bound `phiPlus(K) < b` comes from `phiPlus_lt_of_triangle_with_leProp`.
The lower bound `phiMinus(K) > a` comes from `gtProp a`.

**Key applications**:
* **Kernels**: In the heart SES `0 → ker(f) → E → im(f) → 0`, both `ker(f)` and
  `im(f)` are in the left heart, hence satisfy `gtProp a` and `leProp (a+1)`.
* **Images**: In the heart SES `0 → im(f) → F → coker(f) → 0`, similarly.
**Interval to gtProp.** If all HN phases lie in `(a, b)`, then phiMinus > `a`.

**Extension closure for thin intervals.** In a distinguished triangle
`A → E → B → A[1]` where `A` and `B` are in `P((a, b))` with `b - a ≤ 1`,
then `E ∈ P((a, b))`.

This follows from `phiPlus_lt_of_triangle` and `phiMinus_gt_of_triangle`. It shows
that `P((a, b))` is extension-closed in the triangulated category when `b - a ≤ 1`.
This is a special case of the general extension closure for interval categories.

**Left heart containment (Lemma 4.3a).** If `b - a ≤ 1` and `E ∈ P((a, b))`,
then `E` lies in the heart of the t-structure induced by the slicing shifted by `a`.
This heart is the half-open interval `P((a, a+1])`.

The proof is immediate: phases in `(a, b)` satisfy `> a` (for `gtProp`) and
`< b ≤ a + 1` (for `leProp`).

**Right heart containment (Lemma 4.3b).** If `b - a ≤ 1` and `E ∈ P((a, b))`,
then `E` lies in the heart of the dual half-open t-structure induced by the slicing
shifted by `b - 1`. This heart is the half-open interval `P([b-1, b))`.

Together with `intervalProp_implies_leftHeart`, this establishes the two-heart
embedding: every object in a thin interval lies in both an abelian heart that
controls kernels (left heart) and one that controls cokernels (right heart).

**Right-window bounds.** If `b - a ≤ 1` and `E ∈ P((a, b))`, then `E` satisfies
the phase-window conditions `geProp (b - 1)` and `ltProp b`.

This is the half-open interval `[b - 1, b)` needed for the future right-heart
convention controlling cokernels.

If `f : X ⟶ Y` is an epimorphism in the right heart `P([b - 1, b))` and `X` lies in the
thin interval `P((a, b))`, then `Y` also lies in `P((a, b))`. This is the concrete
cokernel-side closure condition from Bridgeland Lemma 4.2.

If `f : X ⟶ Y` is a monomorphism in the left heart `P((a, a + 1])` and `Y` lies in the
thin interval `P((a, b))`, then `X` also lies in `P((a, b))`. This is the concrete
kernel-side closure condition from Bridgeland Lemma 4.2.
**Interval to leProp.** If all HN phases lie in `(a, b)`, then phiPlus ≤ `b`.

**Phase lower bound from Lemma 3.4.** In a triangle `K → F → Q → K⟦1⟧` with
`F ∈ P(φ)` σ-semistable and `K, Q` in a thin interval `P((a, b))` with `b ≤ a + 1`,
if `Q` is nonzero then `φ ≤ φ⁻(Q)`.

**Phase lower bound from non-strict one-sided containment.** In a triangle
`K → E → Q → K[1]`, if `E` has `φ⁻(E) > a` and `K` satisfies `geProp c` (all phases ≥ c)
with `a < c + 1`, then if `Q` is nonzero, `a < φ⁻(Q)`.

This generalizes `phiMinus_gt_of_triangle_with_gtProp` by replacing `gtProp` (strict)
with `geProp` (non-strict). The key application is cokernel containment via the right
heart, where objects satisfy `geProp(b-1)` rather than `gtProp(b-1)`.

**Phase lower bound from one-sided containment** (dual of
`phiPlus_lt_of_triangle_with_leProp`). In a triangle `K → E → Q → K⟦1⟧`,
if `E` has `φ⁻(E) > a` and `K` satisfies `gtProp c` (all phases > c) with `a < c + 1`,
then if `Q` is nonzero, `a < φ⁻(Q)`.

**Key application**: cokernel/quotient lower bound. In a heart SES, the quotient object
satisfies `gtProp` from heart membership, and this lemma gives the lower phase bound
needed for interval containment.

**Phase upper bound from Lemma 3.4.** In a triangle `K → F → Q → K⟦1⟧` with
`F ∈ P(φ)` σ-semistable and `K, Q` in a thin interval `P((a, b))` with `b ≤ a + 1`
and `φ ∈ (a, b)`, if `K` is nonzero then `φ⁺(K) ≤ φ`.

**Phase upper bound from one-sided containment.** In a triangle `K → E → Q → K⟦1⟧`,
if `E` has `φ⁺(E) < b` and `Q` satisfies `leProp c` (all phases ≤ c) with `c < b + 1`,
then if `K` is nonzero, `φ⁺(K) < b`.

This strengthens `phiPlus_triangle_le` by requiring only a `leProp` bound on `Q` rather
than full `intervalProp`. The condition `c < b + 1` ensures `Q⟦-1⟧` has all phases
`≤ c - 1 < b`, providing the hom-vanishing gap for the coyoneda factoring argument.

**Key application**: kernel containment in the left heart. If `f : E → F` with both
in `P((a, b))`, the heart's SES `ker(f) → E → im(f)` gives a triangle where `im(f)`
is in the heart with `leProp (a + 1)`. Since `a + 1 < b + 1`, this lemma bounds
`φ⁺(ker(f)) < b`, placing the kernel in `P((a, b))`.

**Cokernel/quotient containment in thin intervals (Lemma 4.3 dual).** In a
distinguished triangle `K → E → Q → K[1]` where `E ∈ P((a,b))` and `K` has
`geProp(b-1)` (from right heart membership) and `Q` has `ltProp(b)` (from right
heart membership), then `Q ∈ P((a, b))`.

This is the key property that ensures cokernels computed in the right heart
`P([b-1, b))` stay in the interval `P((a, b))`. Together with
`first_intervalProp_of_triangle` (kernel containment via the left heart), this
establishes that `P((a, b))` is quasi-abelian (Bridgeland Lemma 4.3).

A `k`-linear pretriangulated category is of finite type if all Hom spaces are
finite-dimensional over `k` and for each pair of objects, only finitely many shifted
Hom spaces are nonzero.

The Euler form on objects: `χ(E,F) = Σₙ (-1)ⁿ dim_k Hom(E, F[n])`.
This is defined as a finitely-supported sum using `finsum`.

**Bridgeland's Corollary 1.3** for numerical stability conditions. Each connected
component of `Stab_N(D)` is a complex manifold of dimension `rk(N(D))`.

This is a specialization of the generic class-map theorem to
`v = numericalQuotientMap k C`, which is surjective by definition.

**Bridgeland's Corollary 1.3** for numerical stability conditions. Each connected
component of `Stab_N(D)` is a complex manifold of dimension `rk(N(D))`.

This is a specialization of the generic class-map theorem to
`v = numericalQuotientMap k C`, which is surjective by definition.

**Bridgeland's Corollary 1.3** for `Stab_Λ(D)`. Each connected component of the
stability space `Stab_Λ(D)` is a complex manifold.

The proof is direct: `ComponentTopologicalLinearLocalModel` gives a local homeomorphism
`σ ↦ σ.Z` into `V(Σ) ⊆ Hom(Λ, ℂ)`. Since `Λ` has finite rank, `Hom(Λ, ℂ)` is
finite-dimensional, hence so is `V(Σ)`. The generic manifold construction applies.
A finite-rank class lattice has finite-dimensional complex charge space.

A generic manifold-construction theorem for complex local homeomorphisms into a normed model
space. This is the abstract topological-to-manifold bridge needed to keep Corollary 1.3 small.

A generic manifold-construction theorem for complex local homeomorphisms into a normed model
space. The genuinely nontrivial step is to build a charted space whose transition maps are
restrictions of the identity.

Once a charted space has transition maps in the restriction groupoid, it is automatically a
complex manifold. This is the fully generic part of the manifold assembly.

A Postnikov tower of an object `E` in a pretriangulated category. This consists of
a chain of `n+1` objects connected by `n` distinguished triangles:
```
0 = A₀ → A₁ → ⋯ → Aₙ ≅ E
```
where each step `Aᵢ → Aᵢ₊₁` completes to a distinguished triangle
`Aᵢ → Aᵢ₊₁ → Fᵢ → Aᵢ⟦1⟧` with factor `Fᵢ = (triangle i).obj₃`.

The factor objects are derived from the triangles (as `obj₃`) rather than stored
as a separate data field.

The `i`-th factor of a Postnikov tower, derived as `obj₃` of the `i`-th
distinguished triangle.
The sequence of factor objects in a Postnikov tower.
The number of factors in a Postnikov tower.

A faithful functor that preserves monomorphisms induces an injection on subobject
lattices. If `F : A ⥤ C` is faithful and preserves monomorphisms, subobjects of `E`
in `A` inject into subobjects of `F.obj E` in `C`. Together with `Finite.of_injective`,
this transfers finiteness of subobject lattices from `C` to `A`.

The key use case is the full subcategory inclusion `ι : P(φ) ⥤ C`, where local
finiteness of the slicing gives `Finite (Subobject (ι.obj E))` in `C`, and we
need `Finite (Subobject E)` in `P(φ)`.

Note: fullness is not required. The hypothesis `PreservesMonomorphisms F` is needed
because a faithful functor does not automatically preserve monomorphisms (the mono
test in `C` involves objects not in the image of `F`).

A morphism `f` is *strict* if the canonical comparison morphism from the
(abelian) coimage to the (abelian) image is an isomorphism. In an abelian category,
every morphism is strict.

This uses `Abelian.coimageImageComparison` from `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.Images`,
which is defined without assuming the category is abelian.

An object is *strict-Artinian* if its strict subobjects satisfy the descending chain
condition.
A morphism is a *strict epimorphism* if it is both an epimorphism and strict.
A strict epimorphism is the cokernel of its kernel.
A strict epimorphism that is also mono is an isomorphism.
A strict epimorphism is a normal epimorphism.
A strict epimorphism is a regular epimorphism.
A strict epimorphism is a strong epimorphism.
A morphism is a *strict monomorphism* if it is both a monomorphism and strict.
A strict monomorphism that is also epi is an isomorphism.
A strict monomorphism is the kernel of its cokernel.
A strict monomorphism is a normal monomorphism.

An object is *strict-Noetherian* if its strict subobjects satisfy the ascending chain
condition.

A preadditive category with kernels, cokernels, pullbacks, and pushouts is
*quasi-abelian* if pullbacks of strict epimorphisms are strict epimorphisms and
pushouts of strict monomorphisms are strict monomorphisms.

This follows the definition from Schneiders (1999).

A *strict short exact sequence* is a `ShortComplex` that is short exact (mono left,
epi right, exact in the middle) and where both `f` and `g` are strict morphisms.

In a quasi-abelian category, the kernels of strict epimorphisms are strict monomorphisms,
and the cokernels of strict monomorphisms are strict epimorphisms.
The ordered type of strict subobjects of `X`.

A subobject is *strict* if its arrow is a strict monomorphism. This is the
quasi-abelian notion of admissible / exact subobject used in Bridgeland's finite-length
thin interval categories.
Artinian objects transfer across full faithful functors that preserve monomorphisms.
In an abelian category, strict-Artinian and Artinian coincide, because every mono is strict.
Noetherian objects transfer across full faithful functors that preserve monomorphisms.

In an abelian category, strict-Noetherian and Noetherian coincide, because every mono is
strict.

An object is *strict-Artinian* if its strict subobjects satisfy the descending chain
condition. This is the exact finite-length notion relevant to quasi-abelian categories.

Strict-Artinian objects transfer across full faithful functors that send strict
monomorphisms to strict monomorphisms.

Ordinary Artinian objects are strict-Artinian, since strict subobjects form a subtype of
all subobjects.

The cokernel of any morphism is a strict epimorphism. The canonical comparison
`Coim(cokernel.π g) → Im(cokernel.π g)` is an isomorphism because `cokernel.π g` is epi
(so `Im ≅ cokernel g`), and the coimage `cokernel(kernel.ι(cokernel.π g))` is isomorphic
to `cokernel g` via an explicit inverse pair of `cokernel.desc` morphisms.
In an abelian category, every epimorphism is a strict epimorphism.
If `f` is the cokernel of its kernel, then `f` is a strict epimorphism.
An isomorphism is a strict epimorphism.
A normal epimorphism in a preadditive category with kernels and cokernels is strict.

The kernel of any morphism is a strict monomorphism. The canonical comparison
`Coim(kernel.ι g) → Im(kernel.ι g)` is an isomorphism because `kernel.ι g` is mono
(so `Coim ≅ kernel g`), and the image `kernel(cokernel.π(kernel.ι g))` is isomorphic
to `kernel g` via an explicit inverse pair of `kernel.lift` morphisms.
An isomorphism is a strict monomorphism.
If `f` is the kernel of its cokernel, then `f` is a strict monomorphism.
In an abelian category, every monomorphism is a strict monomorphism.
A normal monomorphism in a preadditive category with kernels and cokernels is strict.

An object is *strict-Noetherian* if its strict subobjects satisfy the ascending chain
condition. This is the exact finite-length notion relevant to quasi-abelian categories.

Strict-Noetherian objects transfer across full faithful functors that send strict
monomorphisms to strict monomorphisms.

Ordinary Noetherian objects are strict-Noetherian, since strict subobjects form a subtype
of all subobjects.
In an abelian category, every morphism is strict.
In an abelian category, every short exact sequence is a strict short exact sequence.
All phases lie between phiMinus and phiPlus.
The highest phase is at least the lowest phase.

The subcategory predicate `P(≥ t)`: an object `E` satisfies this if it is zero or
all phases in some HN filtration of `E` are `≥ t`.
`P(≥ t₂) ≤ P(≥ t₁)` when `t₁ ≤ t₂`.
`P(> t) ⊆ P(≥ t)` (strict lower bound implies non-strict).
Zero objects are in `P(≥ t)` for all `t`.

The subcategory predicate `P(> t)`: an object `E` satisfies this if it is zero or
all phases in some HN filtration of `E` are `> t`.
`P(> t₂) ≤ P(> t₁)` when `t₁ ≤ t₂`.
Zero objects are in `P(> t)` for all `t`.

A morphism from a semistable object of phase `ψ` to an HN-filtered object whose
phases are all strictly less than `ψ` is zero.

A morphism from an HN-filtered object whose phases are all strictly greater than
`ψ` to a semistable object of phase `ψ` is zero.

Morphisms between HN-filtered objects with a phase gap are zero: if every phase
of `Fx` is strictly greater than every phase of `Fy`, then `Hom(X, Y) = 0`.

The subcategory predicate `P(≤ t)`: an object `E` satisfies this if it is zero or
all phases in some HN filtration of `E` are `≤ t`.
`P(≤ t₁) ≤ P(≤ t₂)` when `t₁ ≤ t₂`.
`P(< t) ⊆ P(≤ t)` (strict upper bound implies non-strict).
Zero objects are in `P(≤ t)` for all `t`.

The subcategory predicate `P(< t)`: an object `E` satisfies this if it is zero or
all phases in some HN filtration of `E` are `< t`.
`P(< t₁) ≤ P(< t₂)` when `t₁ ≤ t₂`.
`P((a, b)) ⊆ P(< b)` (interval containment gives strict upper bound).
Zero objects are in `P(< t)` for all `t`.

Auxiliary: any morphism from the `k`-th chain object of an HN filtration (with all
phases strictly greater than those of another filtration) to the target of the second
filtration is zero. Uses the Yoneda exact sequence and `hom_eq_zero_of_gt_phases`.

Auxiliary: any morphism from a semistable object of phase `ψ` to the `k`-th chain
object of an HN filtration (with all phases strictly less than `ψ`) is zero.
Proved by induction on `k`, using the coYoneda exact sequence.

Auxiliary: any morphism from the `k`-th chain object of an HN filtration to a
semistable object of phase `ψ` (with all HN phases strictly greater than `ψ`) is zero.
Proved by induction on `k`, using the Yoneda exact sequence.

paperComment: axiom (c): HN decomposition data for triangulated categories
docstring:
A Harder-Narasimhan (HN) filtration of an object `E` with respect to a phase
predicate `P`. This extends a `PostnikovTower` with phase data: each factor is
semistable with a given phase, and the phases are strictly decreasing.

Drop the first factor from an HN filtration when it is zero. The resulting
filtration has `n - 1` factors with phases `φ(1) > ⋯ > φ(n-1)`.

Drop the last factor from an HN filtration when it is zero. The resulting
filtration has `n - 1` factors with phases `φ(0) > ⋯ > φ(n-2)`.

For any HN filtration of a nonzero object, at least one factor is nonzero.
If all factors were zero, the chain would start and end at zero, contradicting E nonzero.

For any nonzero object, there exists an HN filtration with nonzero first factor.
Proved by repeatedly dropping zero first factors; terminates since `n` decreases
and some factor must be nonzero (by `exists_nonzero_factor`).

For any nonzero object, there exists an HN filtration with nonzero last factor.
Proved by repeatedly dropping zero last factors.
For a nonzero object, any HN filtration has at least one factor.
Transport an HN filtration across an isomorphism `E ≅ E'`.
The lowest phase `φ⁻` of a nonzero object, extracted from a given HN filtration.
The highest phase `φ⁺` of a nonzero object, extracted from a given HN filtration.

Extract the first `k` factors from an HN filtration, giving a filtration
of the `k`-th chain object with phases `φ₀ > ⋯ > φ_{k-1}`.
The prefix filtration has all the original phases up to index `k`.

Shift an HN filtration by `a : ℤ`. If `F` is an HN filtration of `E` with phases
`φ₀ > φ₁ > ⋯`, then `F.shiftHN s a` is an HN filtration of `E⟦a⟧` with phases
`φ₀ + a > φ₁ + a > ⋯`.
The phiMinus of a shifted HN filtration increases by `a`.
The phiPlus of a shifted HN filtration increases by `a`.

A slicing of a pretriangulated category `C`, as defined in
Bridgeland (2007), Definition 5.1. A slicing assigns to each real number `φ`
a full subcategory of semistable objects `P(φ)` (as an `ObjectProperty`),
subject to shift, Hom-vanishing, and Harder-Narasimhan existence axioms.

Each `P(φ)` is an `ObjectProperty C`, enabling use of the `ObjectProperty` API
(e.g. `FullSubcategory`, shift stability, closure properties).

The interval subcategory predicate `P((a,b))`: an object `E` belongs to the
interval subcategory if it is zero or all phases in its HN filtration lie in `(a,b)`.

The intrinsic lowest phase of a nonzero object with respect to a slicing.
This is the phase of the last factor in any HN filtration with nonzero last factor.
Well-defined by `phiMinus_eq_of_nonzero_last_factors`.

The intrinsic highest phase of a nonzero object with respect to a slicing.
This is the phase of the first factor in any HN filtration with nonzero first factor.
Well-defined by `phiPlus_eq_of_nonzero_factors`.
Shifting by `[1]` increases the phase by 1.
Generalized shift: shifting by `n : ℤ` adds `n` to the phase.
The inverse of the shift axiom.
Forward shift by a natural number: if `(P φ) X` then `(P (φ + n)) (X⟦n⟧)`.
Backward shift by a natural number: if `(P (φ + n)) (X⟦n⟧)` then `(P φ) X`.
Zero objects satisfy every phase predicate.
Each phase slice of a slicing contains the zero object.

Extension-closure of `gtProp`: if both `A` and `B` have all HN phases
`> t`, and `A → E → B → A⟦1⟧` is distinguished, then `E` also has all HN
phases `> t`.

The proof uses the Yoneda exact sequence: if `φ⁻(E) ≤ t`, then
`Hom(A, F⁻) = 0` and `Hom(B, F⁻) = 0` (by hom-vanishing), giving
`Hom(E, F⁻) = 0` by exactness, contradicting the nonzero last HN factor.

**Extension-closure of `intervalProp`**: if both `A` and `B` have HN phases in the
open interval `(a, b)`, and `A → E → B → A⟦1⟧` is distinguished, then `E` also has
HN phases in `(a, b)`.

Extension-closure of `leProp`: if both `A` and `B` have all HN phases `≤ t`,
and `A → E → B → A⟦1⟧` is distinguished, then `E` also has all HN phases `≤ t`.

The proof uses the coYoneda exact sequence: if `φ⁺(E) > t`, then
`Hom(F⁺, A) = 0` and `Hom(F⁺, B) = 0` (by hom-vanishing), giving
`Hom(F⁺, E) = 0` by exactness, contradicting the nonzero first HN factor.

Extension-closure of `ltProp`: if both `A` and `B` have all HN phases
`< t`, and `A → E → B → A⟦1⟧` is distinguished, then `E` also has all HN
phases `< t`.

If `A → E → B → A⟦1⟧` is distinguished and `A`, `B` both have
`φ⁻ > t`, then so does `E`. Proved by contradiction using Yoneda exactness.

**Lemma 3.4** (right inequality). In a distinguished triangle `A → E → B → A⟦1⟧`
where the phases of A and B lie in an interval `(a, b)` with `b ≤ a + 1`,
we have `φ⁻(E) ≤ φ⁻(B)`.

The proof uses the Yoneda exact sequence: if `φ⁻(E) > φ⁻(B)`, then maps `E → B⁻`
vanish by hom-vanishing; by exactness, maps `B → B⁻` factor through `A⟦1⟧`, but
A's shifted phases are too high, so all maps `A⟦1⟧ → B⁻` vanish too, giving
`B⁻ = 0`, a contradiction.

Variant of **Lemma 3.4** (`phiMinus_triangle_le`) where the hypothesis `B ∈ P((a,b))`
is weakened to `φ⁺(B) < b`. For a distinguished triangle `A → E → B → A⟦1⟧`
with `A ∈ P((a, b))` and `b ≤ a + 1`, if `φ⁺(B) < b` then `φ⁻(E) ≤ φ⁻(B)`.

If `A → E → B → A⟦1⟧` is distinguished and `A`, `B` both have
`φ⁺ < t`, then so does `E`. Proved by contradiction using coYoneda exactness.

**Lemma 3.4** (left inequality). In a distinguished triangle `A → E → B → A⟦1⟧`
where the phases of A and B lie in an interval `(a, b)` with `b ≤ a + 1`,
we have `φ⁺(A) ≤ φ⁺(E)`.

The width condition `b ≤ a + 1` ensures `B⟦-1⟧` has all phases below any phase of `A`,
so the factoring argument through `B⟦-1⟧` gives a contradiction.

Extend an HN filtration by appending one semistable factor via a distinguished
triangle. Given an HN filtration `G` of `Y'` and a distinguished triangle
`Y' → Z → F → Y'[1]` (where `F` is semistable of phase `ψ` strictly less than
all phases of `G`), produce an HN filtration of `Z` with the additional factor `F`.

The chain object at the split point satisfies `geProp t` if all phases before
the split are ≥ t.

The chain object at the split point satisfies `gtProp t` if all phases before
the split are > t.

The chain object at the split point satisfies `leProp t` if all phases before
the split are ≤ t.

The chain object at the split point satisfies `ltProp t` if all phases before
the split are < t.
The prefix of an HN filtration with phases > t gives a filtration with all phases > t.
The property `P(≥ t)` is closed under isomorphisms.
An HN-filtered object satisfies `geProp t` if all its phases are ≥ t.
The property `P(> t)` is closed under isomorphisms.
An HN-filtered object satisfies `gtProp t` if all its phases are > t.
The property `P(≤ t)` is closed under isomorphisms.
An HN-filtered object satisfies `leProp t` if all its phases are ≤ t.
The property `P(< t)` is closed under isomorphisms.
An HN-filtered object satisfies `ltProp t` if all its phases are < t.

For any nonzero object, there exists an HN filtration with both nonzero first and
last factors. This follows from `exists_nonzero_first` by repeatedly dropping zero
last factors (which preserves the nonzero first factor).

If all maps from `E` to the last factor of an HN filtration are zero,
then the last factor is zero. This is the dual of `isZero_factor_zero_of_hom_eq_zero`,
using `yoneda_exact₃` and hom-vanishing on the shifted prefix filtration.

If `Hom(factor(0), E) = 0` for an HN filtration, then `factor(0)` is zero.
This follows from `chain_hom_eq_zero_of_factor_zero`: all maps from `factor(0)` to
`chain(1)` are zero, and `chain(1) ≅ factor(0)`, so `id = 0`.

For any two HN filtrations of a nonzero object where both have nonzero last factors,
the lowest phases agree. Dual of `phiPlus_eq_of_nonzero_factors`.

The lowest phase of an HN filtration with nonzero last factor is bounded below
by the lowest phase of any other HN filtration. Dual of `phiPlus_le_of_nonzero_factor`.

For any two HN filtrations of a nonzero object where both have nonzero first factors,
the highest phases agree.

The highest phase `φ⁺` of a nonzero HN filtration cannot exceed the highest phase
of any other filtration of the same object, provided the first factor is nonzero.
Proved by contradiction using hom-vanishing and `isZero_factor_zero_of_hom_eq_zero`.

For any nonzero object, there exists an HN filtration whose extreme phases
match the intrinsic `phiPlus` and `phiMinus`. The filtration has nonzero first and
last factors, so `phiPlus_eq` and `phiMinus_eq` apply.
If `t ≤ φ⁻(E)`, then `E ∈ P(≥ t)`.
If `E` has all HN phases `≥ t`, then `E⟦a⟧` has all HN phases `≥ t + ↑a`.
If `t < φ⁻(E)`, then `E ∈ P(> t)`.
If `E` has all HN phases `> t`, then `E⟦a⟧` has all HN phases `> t + ↑a`.

**Interval containment from intrinsic phases.** If `a < φ⁻(E)` and `φ⁺(E) < b`, then
`E ∈ P((a, b))`.
If `φ⁺(E) ≤ t`, then `E ∈ P(≤ t)`.
If `E` has all HN phases `≤ t`, then `E⟦a⟧` has all HN phases `≤ t + ↑a`.
If `φ⁺(E) < t`, then `E ∈ P(< t)`.
If `E` has all HN phases `< t`, then `E⟦a⟧` has all HN phases `< t + ↑a`.

If a semistable object of phase `φ` maps nonzero to `X`, then `φ ≤ φ⁺(X)`. This is the
contrapositive of `hom_eq_zero_of_gt_phases`.

`Slicing.phiMinus` equals `G.φ ⟨G.n - 1, _⟩` for any HN filtration `G`
with nonzero last factor.
If `E ∈ P(≥ t)` and `E` is nonzero, then `t ≤ φ⁻(E)`.

The intrinsic phiMinus is an upper bound on any filtration's phiMinus.
Dual of `phiPlus_le_phiPlus_of_hn`.
If `E ∈ P(> t)` and `E` is nonzero, then `t < φ⁻(E)`.

The intrinsic phiMinus of a nonzero object is bounded below by the lower endpoint of any
interval containing the object.

If `X` maps nonzero to a semistable object of phase `ψ`, then `φ⁻(X) ≤ ψ`. This is the
contrapositive of `hom_eq_zero_of_lt_phases`.
`Slicing.phiMinus ≤ Slicing.phiPlus` for nonzero objects.

The intrinsic phiMinus of a nonzero object is bounded above by the upper endpoint of any
interval containing the object.

`Slicing.phiPlus` equals `G.φ ⟨0, hn⟩` for any HN filtration `G` with nonzero
first factor.

The intrinsic phiPlus of a nonzero object is bounded below by the lower endpoint of any
interval containing the object.
If `E ∈ P(≤ t)` and `E` is nonzero, then `φ⁺(E) ≤ t`.
The intrinsic phiPlus is bounded above by the top phase of any HN filtration.

The intrinsic phiPlus of a nonzero object is bounded above by the upper endpoint of any
interval containing the object.
If `E ∈ P(< t)` and `E` is nonzero, then `φ⁺(E) < t`.

Auxiliary: if `Hom(factor(0), E) = 0` for an HN filtration `F`, then all maps from
`factor(0)` to any chain object are zero. Proved by downward induction on the chain
using the coYoneda exact sequence on the inverted rotation.

An HN filtration with respect to `s.P` can be converted to an HN filtration
with respect to the shifted phase predicate `fun ψ ↦ s.P (ψ + t)`, by subtracting
`t` from all phases.
Construct a 1-factor HN filtration for a semistable object.

Inverse phase shift: given an HN filtration of `E` with respect to
the shifted slicing `(s.phaseShift t).P`, produce an HN filtration with
respect to `s.P` by adding `t` to all phases.
Construct a 0-factor HN filtration on a zero object.
A semistable object of phase `> t` satisfies `gtProp t`.
A semistable object of phase `≤ t` satisfies `leProp t`.

Phase-shifted slicing: `(s.phaseShift t).P ψ = s.P (ψ + t)`. This shifts
all phases down by `t`.

`geProp` of a phase-shifted slicing at cutoff `u` equals `geProp` of the
original slicing at cutoff `u + t`.

`geProp` of a phase-shifted slicing at cutoff `0` equals `geProp` of the
original slicing at cutoff `t`.

`gtProp` of a phase-shifted slicing at cutoff `u` equals `gtProp` of the
original slicing at cutoff `u + t`.

`gtProp` of a phase-shifted slicing at cutoff `0` equals `gtProp` of the
original slicing at cutoff `t`.

`leProp` of a phase-shifted slicing at cutoff `u` equals `leProp` of the
original slicing at cutoff `u + t`.

`leProp` of a phase-shifted slicing at cutoff `0` equals `leProp` of the
original slicing at cutoff `t`.

`ltProp` of a phase-shifted slicing at cutoff `u` equals `ltProp` of the
original slicing at cutoff `u + t`.

`ltProp` of a phase-shifted slicing at cutoff `0` equals `ltProp` of the
original slicing at cutoff `t`.
For a semistable nonzero object, `phiPlus = phiMinus = φ`.

If all factors in an HN filtration have the same phase `φ`, then `E` is
`P(φ)`-semistable. The proof goes by induction along the chain, using
`semistable_of_triangle` at each step and `prop_of_iso` to transfer across
the structural isomorphisms.

**Converse of `phiPlus_eq_phiMinus_of_semistable`**: if `phiPlus(E) = phiMinus(E)` for
a nonzero object, then `E` is semistable of phase `phiPlus(E)`. The proof uses the
single-factor argument: equal extreme phases + strict antitonicity forces `n = 1`,
so the unique HN factor is isomorphic to `E`.

**Extension-closure of the semistable subcategory**. If `A` and `B` are both
`P(φ)`-semistable, and `A → E → B → A⟦1⟧` is a distinguished triangle, then `E`
is also `P(φ)`-semistable. The proof uses `phiPlus_lt_of_triangle` and
`phiMinus_gt_of_triangle` to pin `phiPlus(E) = phiMinus(E) = φ`, then invokes
`semistable_of_phiPlus_eq_phiMinus`.

Any morphism from an object with all HN phases `≥ 0` to one with all HN phases
`< 0` is zero. This is the vanishing convention needed for the right-heart
half-open interval `[0, 1)`.

The hom-vanishing lemma for the right-heart convention at general cutoff `t`:
any morphism from `P(≥ t)` to `P(< t)` is zero.

The hom-vanishing lemma for the t-structure: any morphism from an object with
all HN phases `> 0` to an object with all HN phases `≤ 0` is zero.

The hom-vanishing lemma for phase-shifted slicings at general cutoff `t`:
any morphism from `P(> t)` to `P(≤ t)` is zero.

A t-structure is bounded if every object lies between some `le a` and `ge b` levels.
This is the condition used in Lemma 3.2 and Proposition 5.3 of Bridgeland (2007).

**Splitting at an arbitrary cutoff**. Given an HN filtration `F`
of `E` with all phases in `(a, b)` and a cutoff `t`, produce a
distinguished triangle `X → E → Y → X⟦1⟧` where:
- `X ∈ s.gtProp(t)` (all phases `> t`)
- `Y ∈ s.leProp(t)` (all phases `≤ t`)
- If `X` is nonzero, `φ⁺(X) < b` (preserving the upper interval bound)

Used in the deformation theorem (§7) to split at the τ-semistable phase
while preserving the interval property from `d(σ, τ) < 1`.

**HN filtration splitting at the lower interval bound**. Special case of
`exists_split_at_cutoff` with `t = a`.

Auxiliary: given an HN filtration, produce the dual half-open t-structure
decomposition triangle for the convention `geProp 0` / `ltProp 0`.

A slicing on a triangulated category determines a t-structure.

The convention is:
- `le n = gtProp(-n)`: objects whose HN phases are all `> -n`
- `ge n = leProp(1-n)`: objects whose HN phases are all `≤ 1-n`

This ensures `le 0 = gtProp(0)` (phases `> 0`) and `ge 1 = leProp(0)` (phases `≤ 0`),
with the heart being `P((0,1])`.

A slicing on a triangulated category also determines the dual half-open
t-structure whose heart is `P([0, 1))`.

The convention is:
- `le n = geProp(-n)`: objects whose HN phases are all `≥ -n`
- `ge n = ltProp(1-n)`: objects whose HN phases are all `< 1-n`

This ensures `le 0 = geProp(0)` (phases `≥ 0`) and `ge 1 = ltProp(0)` (phases `< 0`),
with the heart being `P([0,1))`.

**Bounded t-structure from the dual half-open convention.**
The t-structure induced by `toTStructureGE` is bounded.

**Heart identification for the dual half-open convention.**
An object `E` lies in the heart of `toTStructureGE` if and only if it satisfies
`geProp 0` and `ltProp 1`, i.e. its HN phases lie in `[0,1)`.

**Bounded t-structure from slicing.**
The t-structure induced by a slicing is bounded: every object lies between
`le a` and `ge b` for some integers `a, b`.

The proof uses the HN filtration axiom to place every object's phases in
a finite interval, then converts the phase bounds to `le`/`ge` bounds.

**Heart identification.**
An object `E` lies in the heart of the slicing-induced t-structure if and only
if it satisfies both `gtProp 0` (all HN phases > 0) and `leProp 1` (all HN
phases ≤ 1). This identifies the heart with the half-open interval `P((0, 1])`.

**Lemma 6.4 sublemma**. If two stability conditions `σ` and `τ` have the same central
charge `Z`, and a nonzero object `E` is `σ`-semistable of phase `φ` and `τ`-semistable
of phase `ψ` with `|φ - ψ| < 2`, then `φ = ψ`.

Symmetric version: a real multiple of `exp(iπψ)` cannot equal a sum of positive
real multiples of `exp(iπθⱼ)` where all `θⱼ` lie strictly above `ψ` and below `ψ + 1`.

A real multiple of `exp(iπψ)` cannot equal a sum of positive real multiples of
`exp(iπθⱼ)` where all `θⱼ` lie strictly below `ψ` and above `ψ - 1`. The proof takes
the imaginary part after dividing by `exp(iπψ)`: since `sin(π(θⱼ - ψ)) < 0` for all `j`,
the imaginary part of the sum is strictly negative, contradicting the real LHS.

The ordinary compatibility statement for a prestability condition, with the
identity class map simplified away.
Z(σ) has finite σ-seminorm: ‖Z(σ)‖_σ ≤ 1, hence Z(σ) ∈ V(σ).
The generating family of Bridgeland basis neighborhoods on `Stab(D)`.

If the Bridgeland seminorm of `U` is finite and equal to zero, then `U = 0`.
Requires surjectivity of `v` so that vanishing on `cl C v` implies vanishing on all of `Λ`.

An additive homomorphism on `Λ` is zero if it vanishes on all class images and `v` is
surjective. The surjectivity ensures `cl C v` generates all of `Λ`.

Every stability condition admits a small Bridgeland basis neighbourhood contained in its
topological connected component. This is the local connectedness input actually needed later.
Neighborhood-form extraction of a centered Bridgeland basis neighborhood.
Every open neighborhood of `σ` contains a centered Bridgeland basis neighborhood.
**Lemma 6.2**: On a connected component, the finite-seminorm subgroups agree.
The Bridgeland neighborhoods form a topological basis for `Stab(D)`.

paperComment: one direction; apply both ways for full equivalence
docstring: **Lemma 6.2**: On a connected component, seminorms are equivalent (domination).

Connected components in `StabilityCondition C` are open, because small Bridgeland basis
neighbourhoods stay inside the connected component of their centre.
Two stability conditions with same Z and d < 1 are equal (Lemma 6.4).

Two stability conditions lying in the same Bridgeland basis neighborhood of `σ`
and with the same central charge are equal.

A local `ε₀ < 1 / 10` witness for Theorem 7.1, obtained by shrinking the standard
`exists_epsilon0` witness.

A small deformation of the central charge lifts directly to a point of `basisNhd C σ ε`.

This is the form used in the topology arguments for Theorem 1.2: it gives both the
prescribed central charge and the exact `basisNhd` control, so no radius enlargement
is needed after applying the deformation theorem.
Shrinking the radius at a fixed center shrinks the Bridgeland basis neighborhood.
A basis neighborhood contains its center.

A small Bridgeland basis neighborhood, with radius below the local Theorem 7.1 witness,
lies in the connected component of its center. This is the direct straight-line interpolation
argument from Bridgeland §7.

If `τ ∈ B_ε(σ)`, then some smaller basis neighborhood of `τ` is contained in `B_ε(σ)`.

This is the local basis-refinement step used later in the local homeomorphism proof.
It only needs seminorm domination for nearby stability conditions.

Local continuation along the straight-line charge interpolation inside a fixed basis
neighborhood. Starting from any lifted point `ρ₀` over time `t`, nearby times also admit lifts
inside the same ambient basis neighborhood and in the same connected component as `ρ₀`.
The affine interpolation between the central charges of `σ` and `τ`.
Local forward domination inside a Bridgeland basis neighborhood.

A local form of Bridgeland's Lemma 6.2: on a single basis neighborhood, the
center seminorm is dominated by the nearby seminorm with a finite constant.
Wide-sector finite length is monotone under shrinking `ε₀`.

A Bridgeland prestability condition on `C`, viewed as the specialization of
the class-map theory to the identity map `K₀(C) → K₀(C)`.

A Bridgeland prestability condition with respect to a class map
`v : K₀(C) → Λ`. The central charge lives on `Λ`, and the ordinary ambient
charge is recovered by precomposition with `v`.
The central charge evaluated at the class of `E`. This is `Z(v[E])`.

Compatibility: for nonzero semistable `E` of phase `φ`, `σ.charge E`
lies on the ray `ℝ₊ · exp(iπφ)`.

A Bridgeland stability condition on `C`, viewed as the specialization of the
class-map theory to the identity map `K₀(C) → K₀(C)`.

A Bridgeland stability condition with respect to a class map `v : K₀(C) → Λ`.
This is the locally-finite refinement of `PreStabilityCondition.WithClassMap`.

The local-homeomorphism package for connected components of `Stab_v(D)`, stated directly in
terms of the class-map charge `Z : Λ →+ ℂ`. Specializing to `v = id` recovers Bridgeland's
Theorem 1.2 proposition-object; specializing to the numerical quotient recovers Corollary 1.3.
The type of `v`-relative stability conditions in a fixed connected component.
The connected-component index set for `Stab_v(D)`.

The Bridgeland topology on `Stab_v(D)`, generated by the basis neighborhoods
`B_ε(σ)` for all stability conditions `σ` and all `ε ∈ (0, 1/8)`.

This is the BLMNPS topology: the coarsest making both the charge map `σ ↦ σ.Z`
and the slicing map continuous. When `v = id`, this recovers Bridgeland's original
topology on `Stab(D)`.
The basis neighborhood `B_ε(σ)` for the Bridgeland topology on `Stab_v(D)`.

The imaginary part of `m · exp(iπψ) · exp(-iπφ)` is `m · sin(π(ψ - φ))`.
This is the core identity underlying all phase-sign arguments in the Bridgeland
deformation theory (Lemmas 6.1–6.4 and the HN existence proof).

The Bridgeland generalized metric on slicings. For slicings `s₁` and `s₂`,
this is the supremum over all nonzero objects `E` of
`max(|φ₁⁺(E) - φ₂⁺(E)|, |φ₁⁻(E) - φ₂⁻(E)|)`.

The Bridgeland seminorm `‖U‖_σ` on `Hom(Λ, ℂ)`. For a class-map stability
condition `σ = (Z, P)` with class map `v : K₀(D) → Λ` and a group homomorphism
`U : Λ → ℂ`, this is `sup { |U(v[E])| / |Z(v[E])| : E is σ-semistable and nonzero }`.

When `v = id` (i.e., `Λ = K₀(D)`), this recovers Bridgeland's original seminorm.

The ordinary compatibility statement for a stability condition, with the
identity class map simplified away.

A reusable non-existential package for the current formalization of Bridgeland's
Theorem 1.2 on a fixed connected component.
The restricted central charge map attached to a component local model.

**Bridgeland's Theorem 1.2.** For each connected component Σ of the stability space,
there is a linear subspace V(Σ) ⊂ Hom(Λ, ℂ) with a normed topology and a local homeomorphism
Z : Σ → V(Σ) mapping each stability condition to its central charge.
The restricted Bridgeland seminorm is a genuine additive norm on `V(Σ)`.
The Bridgeland norm on `V(Σ)` attached to a chosen representative of the component.

The chosen norm on `V(Σ)` is equivalent to the Bridgeland norm coming from any
representative `σ ∈ Σ`. This is the formal version of Bridgeland's statement that the norms
`‖·‖_σ` on a connected component are equivalent.
A chosen representative of a connected component of `StabilityCondition.WithClassMap C v`.
The seminorm balls in `V(Σ)` coming from the representative `σ₀ ∈ Σ`.
The old seminorm balls are exactly the metric balls for the induced norm on `V(Σ)`.
The basis of seminorm balls defining the topology on `V(Σ)`.
Bridgeland's `V(Σ)`, implemented using a chosen representative of the component.
The linear topology on `V(Σ)` generated by seminorm balls for one representative.

The ad hoc generated topology on `V(Σ)` agrees with the topology induced by the Bridgeland
norm defined above.

Any element of the chosen `V(Σ)` has finite Bridgeland seminorm with respect to any
stability condition in the same connected component.
The component of stability conditions with connected-component label `cc`.

The canonical componentwise local linear model used to state Bridgeland's
Theorem 1.2 in terms of an explicit normed complex vector space `V(Σ)`.
The central charge map restricted to a connected component and landing in `V(Σ)`.
For `σ ∈ Σ`, its central charge lies in `V(Σ)`.
The old seminorm-ball basis is a genuine topological basis for the norm topology on `V(Σ)`.

The subspace `V(σ)` of group homomorphisms with finite seminorm.
This is an `AddSubgroup` of `Λ →+ ℂ` consisting of those `U` for which
`‖U‖_σ < ∞`. On a connected component of `Stab(D)`, this subspace is independent
of the chosen `σ` (by Lemma 6.2).

**Proposition 6.3** (V(Σ) equality for nearby stability conditions). If
`τ ∈ B_ε(σ)` with `ε < 1/8`, then `V(σ) = V(τ)`. This shows the subgroup `V`
is locally constant on `Stab(D)`, hence constant on connected components.

The forward direction uses `stabSeminorm_le_of_near`. The reverse direction uses
the explicit bound to show `‖σ.Z - τ.Z‖_τ < cos(πε)`, then applies the
comparison with `σ` and `τ` swapped. The key inequality is
`sin(πε) · (1 + cos(πε)) < cos²(πε)` for `ε < 1/8`, proved via
`sin(x) < x` and `x² + 2x < 1` for `x = πε < π/8 < √2 - 1`.

**Proposition 6.3 (same Z case)**. If `σ` and `τ` have the same central charge
and `d(P, Q) < ε < 1/2`, then the finite seminorm subgroups agree: `V(σ) = V(τ)`.
This is the key ingredient for showing `V(Σ)` is well-defined on connected components.

paperComment: pseudo-emetric (not generalized metric: missing separation); Lean uses sup of phase differences, paper uses inf of containment radii — equivalence not proved
docstring:
The Bridgeland generalized metric makes `Slicing C` a pseudo-extended-metric space.
This is **not** the topology used on `StabilityCondition.WithClassMap C v` (which combines `slicingDist`
with the seminorm `stabSeminorm`); it is the intrinsic metric on slicings alone.

**Lemma 6.1** (one direction). If the slicing distance `d(P, Q) < ε`, then every
`Q`-semistable object of phase `φ` has all `P`-HN phases in the interval `(φ - ε, φ + ε)`.
In terms of intrinsic phases: `|φ⁺_P(E) - φ| < ε` and `|φ⁻_P(E) - φ| < ε`.

**Node 6.2d**: For a `τ`-semistable nonzero object `E` of phase `φ` with
`d(σ, τ) < ε < 1/2`, the `σ`-HN width of `E` is less than `2ε`, so the sector
bound applies. Combined with the seminorm bound on `W - Z`, this controls
`‖Z([E])‖` by `‖W([E])‖` (where `W = τ.Z` and `Z = σ.Z`).

If the slicing distance is less than `ε`, the intrinsic `φ⁻` values are within `ε`.
This is one direction of **Lemma 6.1**.

If the slicing distance is less than `ε`, the intrinsic `φ⁺` values are within `ε`.
This is one direction of **Lemma 6.1**.

**Sector bound (Lemma 6.2 core)**. For a stability condition `σ = (Z, P)` and a group
homomorphism `U : Λ →+ ℂ`, if every semistable factor satisfies
`‖U([A])‖ ≤ M · ‖Z([A])‖`, then the bound extends to any object `E` with narrow HN width:
`‖U([E])‖ ≤ (M / cos(πη/2)) · ‖Z([E])‖`, where `η` bounds the HN phase width.

The proof decomposes `E` via its HN filtration (a PostnikovTower with phase data),
applies K₀ additivity, the pointwise seminorm bound on factors, and the
sector estimate `norm_sum_exp_ge_cos_mul_sum`.
Sector bound using intrinsic phase width `phiPlus - phiMinus`.

The generalized metric is at most `ε` if both `φ⁺` and `φ⁻` differences are bounded
by `ε` for all nonzero objects. This is the "converse" direction of the phase-bound lemmas
`phiPlus_sub_lt_of_slicingDist` and `phiMinus_sub_lt_of_slicingDist`.
The Bridgeland generalized metric is zero on the diagonal: `d(P, P) = 0`.
The Bridgeland generalized metric is symmetric: `d(P, Q) = d(Q, P)`.
The Bridgeland generalized metric satisfies the triangle inequality.
For σ-semistable nonzero `A`, the ratio `‖U(A)‖/‖Z(A)‖` is bounded by the seminorm.

**General Lemma 6.2** (explicit seminorm bound). If `d(P, Q) < ε < 1/2`
and `‖τ.Z - σ.Z‖_σ < cos(πε)`, then for any `U` with finite `σ`-seminorm, the
`τ`-seminorm is bounded: `‖U‖_τ ≤ ‖U‖_σ / (cos(πε) - ‖τ.Z - σ.Z‖_σ)`.

This is the quantitative core of the seminorm comparison, used for both
`stabSeminorm_lt_top_of_near` and the reverse direction of **Proposition 6.3**.

**General Lemma 6.2** (seminorm finiteness comparison). If `d(P, Q) < ε < 1/2`
and `‖τ.Z - σ.Z‖_σ < cos(πε)`, then `V(σ) ⊆ V(τ)`.

**Seminorm comparison for same central charge** (**Lemma 6.2** consequence).
If `σ` and `τ` have the same central charge and `d(P, Q) < ε < 1/2`, then
`‖U‖_τ < ⊤` whenever `‖U‖_σ < ⊤`. This shows `V(σ) ⊆ V(τ)`, and by symmetry
`V(σ) = V(τ)` for nearby stability conditions with the same central charge.

The seminorm is nonneg: `stabSeminorm σ U ≥ 0`. This is trivially true since
`ℝ≥0∞` values are nonneg, but useful for API.
The seminorm at zero is zero.

**Lemma 6.4 for P-semistable objects**. If `σ` and `τ` have the same central charge,
`d(P, Q) < 1`, `E` is nonzero and `τ`-semistable of phase `φ`, and `E` is also
`σ`-semistable of some phase `c`, then `c = φ` and `E` is `σ`-semistable of phase `φ`.

This handles the case of Lemma 6.4 where `E` has a trivial `σ`-HN filtration (single
factor), combining the metric phase bound with `phase_eq_of_same_Z`.

**One-sided phase impossibility for Lemma 6.4** (above). Symmetric version of
`false_of_all_hn_phases_below`: if all nonzero factors have phase strictly above `φ`
(and below `φ + 1`), we also reach a contradiction.

**One-sided phase impossibility for Lemma 6.4** (below). If `σ` and `τ` have the same
central charge, `E` is nonzero and `τ`-semistable of phase `φ`, and all nonzero factors
in a `σ`-HN filtration of `E` have phase strictly below `φ` (and above `φ - 1`), then
we reach a contradiction.

The proof decomposes `Z(E) = Σ Z(Fᵢ)` via K₀ additivity, divides by `exp(iπφ)`,
and shows the imaginary part is both zero (since `Z(E) = m · exp(iπφ)` is on a ray)
and strictly negative (since each nonzero factor contributes a negative `sin` term).

**Cross-slicing imaginary part contradiction**. If `σ.Z = τ.Z`, `X` is nonzero,
all `σ`-HN phases of `X` are in `(φ, φ + 1)`, and all `τ`-HN phases are in `(φ - 1, φ]`,
then `False`.

The proof decomposes `Z(X)` via both `σ`- and `τ`-HN filtrations. From the `σ`-decomposition,
`Im(Z(X)/exp(iπφ)) > 0`. From the `τ`-decomposition, `Im(Z(X)/exp(iπφ)) ≤ 0`.

**One-sided phase impossibility** (below with equality). If `σ` and `τ` have the same
central charge, `E` is `τ`-semistable at `φ`, and all `σ`-HN phases are `≤ φ` with at
least one strictly below, then `False`. This extends `false_of_all_hn_phases_below`
to allow some phases equal to `φ`.

**Phase straddling for Lemma 6.4** (intrinsic). If `σ.Z = τ.Z`, `d(P, Q) < 1`,
`E` is nonzero and `τ`-semistable of phase `φ`, then the intrinsic `σ`-phases satisfy
`phiMinus_σ(E) ≤ φ ≤ phiPlus_σ(E)`.

Combined with `phiPlus_σ(E), phiMinus_σ(E) ∈ (φ-1, φ+1)` from
`intervalProp_of_semistable_slicingDist`, this pins `φ` between the extreme `σ`-phases.
The proof applies `false_of_all_hn_phases_below` / `false_of_all_hn_phases_above` to
the canonical HN filtration from `exists_both_nonzero`.

**Bridgeland's Lemma 6.4** (statement and proof). If two stability conditions have the
same central charge and `d(P, Q) < 1`, then their slicings agree on all phases.

The proof proceeds as follows. Given `E ∈ Q(φ)`, we show `E ∈ P(φ)`:
1. Split `E` via `σ`'s t-structure at `φ`: `X → E → Y → X⟦1⟧` with
   `X ∈ P(>φ)` and `Y ∈ P(≤φ)`, all σ-phases in `(φ-1, φ+1)`.
2. Show `τ.phiPlus(X) ≤ φ` by contradiction: if `τ`'s top factor `U` of `X` had
   phase `> φ`, then `Hom(U, E) = 0` (τ-hom-vanishing) forces `U → X` to factor
   through `Y⟦-1⟧`, but `Hom(U, Y⟦-1⟧) = 0` too, giving `U → X = 0`,
   contradicting `U ≠ 0`.
3. Now `X` has σ-phases `> φ` and τ-phases `≤ φ`: the cross-slicing imaginary
   part argument (`false_of_gt_and_le_phases`) gives `X = 0`.
4. So `E ≅ Y` has all σ-phases `≤ φ`. The imaginary part argument then forces
   all phases `= φ`, making `E` σ-semistable at `φ`.

**Auxiliary**: for a nonzero object with `s.gtProp φ`, the intrinsic minimum phase
is `> φ`.

For a σ-semistable nonzero object of phase `ψ`, the imaginary part of
`Z(F) · exp(-iπφ)` equals `b · sin(π(ψ - φ))` for some `b > 0`. This factors
out the repeated "divide by exp, rewrite to sin" computation in the Lemma 6.4 proofs.

**Auxiliary**: for a nonzero object with `s.leProp φ`, the intrinsic maximum phase
is `≤ φ`.

A stability function on an abelian category `A` assigns to each object a complex number
such that nonzero objects map into the semi-closed upper half plane, and the assignment
is additive on short exact sequences (Bridgeland, Definition 2.1).

We use an object-level function `Zobj : A → ℂ` rather than a K₀-level homomorphism,
since the Grothendieck group `K₀` in the current formalization is defined only for
pretriangulated categories, not abelian categories.

An object `E` of an abelian category is *semistable* with respect to a stability
function `Z` if it is nonzero and for every nonzero subobject `A ↪ E`, the phase of
`A` is at most the phase of `E` (Bridgeland, Definition 2.2).

An object is *stable* if it is nonzero and every nonzero proper subobject has
strictly smaller phase.
The cokernel of `ofLE ⊥ S _` is isomorphic to `(S : A)`.
`ofLE ⊥ S h` is the zero morphism (since the source is a zero object).

`Z` sends isomorphic objects to the same value. Uses additivity on the
short exact sequence `0 → E → F → 0` induced by the isomorphism.

A nonzero object that is not semistable has a destabilizing subobject: a nonzero
subobject with strictly larger phase.

Among all nonzero subobjects of a nonzero object with finite subobject lattice,
there exists one with maximal phase.

In an Artinian object, repeated destabilizing subobjects terminate. Hence every nonzero
subobject contains a semistable subobject of at least the same phase. This is the first
chain-condition step in Bridgeland's Proposition 2.4.
In a non-semistable Artinian object, there is a semistable destabilizing subobject.
`IsSemistable` is invariant under isomorphisms.

Given B : Subobject E and C : Subobject (B : A), lift C to a subobject of E
by composing the arrows.
Lifting ⊥ gives ⊥.
The lifted subobject is below B.
Lifting a nonzero subobject gives a nonzero subobject.

If `M` has maximal phase among all nonzero subobjects of `E`, then `M` is
semistable as an object. This is because any destabilizing subobject of `(M : A)`
lifts to a nonzero subobject of `E` with higher phase, contradicting maximality.

The max-phase subobject `M` is not `⊤` when `E` is not semistable and `M ≠ ⊤`,
which happens when `φ(M) > φ(E)`. Since `φ(⊤) = φ(E)` (via the iso `⊤ ≅ E`),
`φ(M) > φ(E)` implies `M ≠ ⊤`.

**Phase see-saw (lower bound)**: For a short exact sequence `0 → A → B → C → 0`

This follows from `Z(B) = Z(A) + Z(C)` and `min_arg_le_arg_add`.

The phase of an object `E`, defined as `(1/π) · arg(Z(E))`.
For a nonzero object, `Z(E) ∈ upperHalfPlaneUnion` implies `arg(Z(E)) ∈ (0, π]`,

Phase is invariant under isomorphisms.

**Phase see-saw (upper bound)**: For a short exact sequence `0 → A → B → C → 0`
with `A, C` nonzero, the phase of `B` is at most the maximum of the phases of `A` and `C`.
This follows from `Z(B) = Z(A) + Z(C)` and `arg_add_le_max`.
The phase of the lifted subobject equals the phase of C.

A Harder-Narasimhan filtration of a nonzero object `E` in an abelian category,

This is a finite chain of subobjects `0 = E₀ < E₁ < ⋯ < Eₙ = E` with:
- `n ≥ 1` factors
- the phases are strictly decreasing
- each factor `Eᵢ₊₁/Eᵢ` is semistable with phase `φ i`

The semistability condition connects the chain to the stability function `Z`:

We encode this via `factor_semistable` and `factor_phase`, where the factor

**Proposition 2.3** (Bridgeland): HN filtrations are essentially unique.
When all objects have finite subobject lattices, any two HN filtrations of the
same object have the same number of semistable factors.

The proof shows that `chain(1)` of any HN filtration equals the maximally
destabilizing subobject (MDS), which is intrinsic to the object. The key step
is the semistable descent lemma: any semistable subobject with phase `> φ(0)`
must be zero (by hom-vanishing to each factor). This forces the MDS phase to
equal `φ(0)`, and the MDS to equal `chain(1)`. We then quotient and induct.

The intrinsic lowest phase of a nonzero object `E` with respect to a
stability function `Z`, assuming finite subobject lattices. This is the phase of

`phiMinus` equals `F.φ ⟨F.n - 1, _⟩` for any HN filtration `F`.
`phiMinus ≤ phiPlus` for nonzero objects.

The intrinsic highest phase of a nonzero object `E` with respect to a

`phiPlus` equals `F.φ ⟨0, F.hn⟩` for any HN filtration `F`.
For semistable objects, `phiPlus = phiMinus = Z.phase E`.

**Heart abelianity.** The heart of a t-structure on a triangulated category is abelian,
assuming the heart has finite products.

**Heart abelianity (canonical form).** The full subcategory of heart objects of a
t-structure on a triangulated category is abelian.

**Admissibility of heart morphisms.** Every morphism in the heart is admissible: for
`f₁ : X₁ → X₂` in the heart, the cone `X₃` of `ι.map f₁` decomposes as
`(ι K)⟦1⟧ → X₃ → ι Q` via the truncation triangle, with `K, Q` in the heart.
The biproduct of two heart objects lies in the heart.
The heart of a t-structure is closed under binary products.
The heart of a t-structure is closed under finite products.
The zero object lies in the heart of any t-structure.
The full subcategory defined by the heart has finite products.

**No negative Hom spaces in the heart.** For heart objects `X` and `Y`, every morphism
`ι X ⟶ (ι Y)⟦n⟧` is zero when `n < 0`.
